description: The BSA replaces the Indian Evidence Act (IEA) of 1872. It governs the admissibility, relevance, and evaluation of evidence in Indian courts.
key_changes_from_iea: Electronic records explicitly recognized as primary evidence
Expanded definition of 'document' to include electronic records
Certificate requirements for electronic evidence updated
Oral evidence via video conferencing explicitly permitted
Expert opinion on electronic evidence included
Admissibility of statements made in electronic form

important_sections.56: Facts of which evidence need not be given — judicial notice
important_sections.57: Facts admitted need not be proved
important_sections.59: Proof of facts — oral, documentary, or electronic
important_sections.63: Admissibility of electronic records — certificate requirement
important_sections.64: Primary evidence — original document or record
important_sections.65: Secondary evidence — certified copies, oral accounts
important_sections.75: Opinion of expert — science, art, handwriting, fingerprint, electronic form
important_sections.76: Facts bearing upon opinions of experts
important_sections.81: Presumption as to documents produced as record of evidence
important_sections.82: Presumption as to document produced as record of evidence in criminal cases
important_sections.85: Presumption as to Gazettes, newspapers, and private documents
important_sections.86: Presumption as to powers of attorney
important_sections.87: Presumption as to certified copies of foreign judicial records
important_sections.88: Presumption as to books, maps, and charts
important_sections.90: Presumption as to messages forwarded by telecommunication
